headline_verdict: Coherent architecture, but file-count justification requires boundary review
primary_review_question: Do we need 326 files to pull off this taxonomy scanning and swarming solution?

file_economy.score_meaning: Mostly justified; review zero-method and one-method boundaries before scaling.

category_rows.notes: Justified when API, browser, route, and integration boundaries stay navigable.
Justified when command behavior and operator entry points stay isolated.
Justified when each module owns one runtime responsibility.
Justified when report rendering stays isolated from scoring and healing.
Review for helper fragmentation and repeated one-method modules.
Justified when evaluator pieces remain independently testable.
Justified when scanning, extraction, evidence, and healing stay separable.
Justified when tests protect coherence governance and report contracts.
Justified when decomposition keeps agent work packets small and governable.
Justified only for wrappers, config, registry, boundary, or executable surfaces.
Review whether the single method has durable semantic weight.

final_verdict:
The studio codebase now tells a coherent taxonomy story. The scan reviewed 326 files, found file anchors on all 326, found all 639 expected method anchors, and reported 100/100 coherence.

That means the codebase is semantically trustworthy at the taxonomy level. However, the file count should not be treated as automatically justified simply because coherence is 100/100.

The recommendation is to accept the taxonomy coherence result, preserve the responsibility-first architecture, and run a file-economy review pass before scaling the pattern to larger Python or LLC codebases.
